spec_match.rationale: Reads `navigator.cpuPerformance?.tier` directly and falls back to a transparent blend of hardwareConcurrency + deviceMemory + a sieve micro-benchmark — exactly the layered fallback the WICG explainer recommends for graceful adoption.

interactive.rationale: Tier-pretend buttons, an auto-detect button, a live JSON config dispatcher, a policy table with match highlighting, and a tier gauge — well past the static-card bar.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises a policy table mapping levels onto product flags and a JSON config that recomputes live as the tier changes; the page delivers both with eight realistic feature flags.

edge_cases.rationale: Origin-trial/flag-missing path is handled by falling back to the heuristic, but no demo of: malformed/zero values from the legacy hints, the API returning a string the policy table doesn't understand, or PermissionsPolicy gating the cpu API in an iframe.

references.rationale: See-also section includes https://chromestatus.com/feature/5189864286978048 and the WICG explainer.

design_system.rationale: All colours via CSS variables, tier-btn override pairs background AND color (var(--text-black) / var(--bg-ivory)), gauge fill uses --accent-blue, no static-card pattern.

openQuestions.title: Show what a non-matching tier value does
openQuestions.detail: If `navigator.cpuPerformance?.tier` returns something unexpected (e.g. an integer scale 0-1 rather than 'low/mid/high'), the policy `row[tier]` lookup will return undefined and the JSON config will silently emit nulls. Surface that failure mode with a small 'inject unknown tier' button so visitors learn to defensive-code their lookup.

openQuestions.title: Add a Permissions-Policy iframe variant
openQuestions.detail: Page-level feature gating (`Permissions-Policy: cpu=()`) hides the API. Include an iframe variant or a worked example showing how the policy router degrades when the API is policy-blocked vs simply missing.

openQuestions.title: Cite where the policy table comes from
openQuestions.detail: The eight feature flags in the policy table are illustrative but a real product would derive them from product analytics. Either credit the example as illustrative or link to a real-world case study (Figma, Photoshop Web, Discord) that publishes a tier policy.

summary: The strongest concept in this folder — handles the API + fallback elegantly, dispatches a live JSON config, and exposes the policy as the editable artifact it really is. Just add an 'unknown tier' edge case and a PermissionsPolicy variant.
